title: The Chokepoint Wars
subhead: The Hormuz closure exposed the myth of energy security built on maritime lanes. Two months in, the Gulf's pipeline workarounds are maxed out, OPEC has fractured, and the petrodollar architecture is being stress-tested by a new reality: the country that built the most sophisticated blockade in history is being blockaded itself.

sections.heading: The Hormuz Stress Test: Pipelines Can't Replace Geography
sections.body:
Two months into the Iran war, the Strait of Hormuz remains largely shut. The bypass infrastructure that Gulf states built over decades—the Saudi Petroline, the UAE's Abu Dhabi Crude Oil Pipeline (Adcop), Iraq's Kirkuk-Ceyhan line—is running at capacity. It is not enough.

The numbers are stark. Petroline can move up to 7 million barrels per day from Saudi's eastern fields to Yanbu on the Red Sea, but the loading terminals at Yanbu were never designed for this throughput. Iranian drones have already hit pumping stations, temporarily knocking 700,000 barrels offline. The Sumed pipeline from Yanbu to Egypt—necessary for Europe-bound oil—caps at 2.5 million barrels daily. The UAE's Adcop offers 1.5 million barrels of relief to Fujairah on the Gulf of Oman, but Fujairah itself was struck by Iranian drones three times in March, setting storage tanks ablaze.

The real losers are the states without alternatives. Kuwait's 2 million barrels per day of exports all went through Hormuz. Kuwait Petroleum declared force majeure in March and extended it in April—the company says even if Hormuz reopens, production won't recover for months. Iraq lost nearly all of its 3.4 million daily barrels of southern exports; the northern Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey can only carry 250,000. Qatar's 77 million tonnes of annual LNG capacity at Ras Laffan—19% of global trade—has no workaround at all. No Qatari tanker has passed Hormuz in 60 days.

The lesson is structural: geography cannot be circumvented by infrastructure, only mitigated. Every pipeline terminal, every storage facility, every loading dock is itself a target. The Gulf's bypass capacity of 3.5–5.5 million barrels per day covers roughly a quarter of pre-war Hormuz flows. The rest is trapped.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Gulf states would have been building far more bypass capacity years ago—but that would have required acknowledging that the security umbrella provided by the US Navy might not hold forever. The trap was believing in permanent maritime security. The cost is now measured in billions of dollars of stranded crude and reputations as reliable suppliers.

sections.heading: The Two-Way Blockade: Iran's Oil Industry Under Siege
sections.body:
While Iran chokes world oil supplies by closing Hormuz, the United States is choking Iran's oil industry with a counter-blockade. The squeeze is now operational: Iran pumped over 3 million barrels per day before the war, but with the US Treasury ratcheting sanctions and the US military seizing tankers off Asia, Iran has nowhere to send its oil.

Storage is filling up. Kpler estimates Iran has about two weeks of storage capacity left at current production levels. Wood Mackenzie gives it three weeks. Once storage fills, Iran must shut in production. Analysts note that Iran's aging wells—poorly maintained after 47 years of sanctions—may not restart easily. The 1979 revolution saw oil output collapse from 6 million to 1.5 million barrels daily when workers struck; the industry never fully recovered.

The squeeze is already visible. Satellite imagery shows slower storage filling at Kharg Island, Iran's main export terminal—suggesting production cuts are underway. Iran appears to be storing oil in tankers positioned around Kharg, but those tankers cannot leave. The US has seized at least two vessels carrying Iranian crude in Asian waters.

Iran built its own Hormuz bypass—the Goreh-Jask pipeline with a design capacity of 1 million barrels per day—but sanctions and unfinished infrastructure kept actual flows under 70,000 barrels daily before the war. In two months of conflict, Kpler data shows only a single tanker has loaded at Jask.

The structural irony is complete: Iran weaponized the chokepoint it depends on most. The closure that was supposed to pressure the West is now pressuring Iran's own economic lifeline. Hardliners on Iranian state TV have discussed the storage crisis openly—unprecedented acknowledgment of vulnerability.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for the US is patience: let storage fill, let wells shut, let the economic pain compound. The trap would be overreaching—military strikes on Iranian oil infrastructure that unite domestic opinion and give Tehran a rallying cause. The cost of miscalculation is a longer war and a permanently damaged Iranian oil sector that destabilizes the regime.

sections.heading: OPEC Fractures: The UAE Walks Away
sections.body:
On April 29, the United Arab Emirates announced its withdrawal from OPEC and OPEC+, effective May 1. The decision brings years of tensions with Saudi Arabia into the open. The UAE—pumping over 4 million barrels per day and possessing significant spare capacity—is no longer bound by Riyadh's production quotas.

The timing is not accidental. The Iran war has created a global supply deficit that no voluntary production cut was designed to address. With Hormuz shut and prices above $120 per barrel, the UAE calculates that maximizing output serves its national interest far better than adhering to a cartel agreement designed for a different era. Abu Dhabi prioritizes autonomy over Riyadh-led oil governance.

The split exposes deeper Gulf dynamics. The UAE has spent years building the Fujairah oil hub on the Gulf of Oman—outside Hormuz—as an export terminal. That infrastructure is now proving its strategic value. While Saudi Arabia remains the world's only swing producer with meaningful spare capacity, the UAE has positioned itself as an independent high-volume supplier with geographic advantages that Riyadh cannot match.

Analysts see this as a strategic break in Gulf power dynamics. What we're witnessing is not just an OPEC member leaving—it's the second-largest Gulf producer calculating that cartel discipline is a constraint, not an asset, in an era of supply shocks. The UAE's exit raises questions about whether OPEC's share of world crude production—already falling—can sustain cartel discipline when the next crisis hits.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for the UAE is to pump while prices are high and geography favors it. The trap for Saudi Arabia is losing its closest partner and watching OPEC's market influence erode from within. The cost for oil markets is the loss of coordinated supply management just when it's needed most.

sections.heading: Europe's Stagflation Trap: The ECB's Impossible Choice
sections.body:
Eurozone inflation jumped to 3% in April, up from 2.6% in March, driven by a 10.9% surge in energy prices. Crude is trading above $120 per barrel—up from $73 before the war began on February 28. First-quarter growth came in at 0.1%. The combination of rising prices and stalled growth has policymakers invoking the word they hoped never to use again: stagflation.

ECB President Christine Lagarde insisted the term belongs to the 1970s and does not apply today. But the structural parallel is uncomfortable. The twin oil shocks of 1973 and 1979—the Arab embargo and the Iranian revolution—created exactly this dynamic: supply-driven inflation that central banks cannot fix by raising rates because the root cause is not excess demand but constrained supply.

The ECB held rates at 2% this week, as did the Bank of England, the Bank of Japan, and the US Federal Reserve. All are trapped. Raising rates chokes credit and deepens the growth slowdown. Cutting rates fuels inflation. Holding steady watches both problems worsen. European policymakers are facing the cost of energy dependence they never fully resolved after Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

The difference now: the Hormuz closure affects not just Europe but Asia, the Americas, and every economy that relies on Gulf crude. Jet fuel shortages are already forcing flight cancellations. The inflation is not a regional problem—it is a global price shock transmitted through the energy system.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move would have been building strategic reserves and diversifying supply sources years ago—a lesson Europe should have learned in 2022. The trap is believing that monetary policy can solve supply-side inflation. The cost is a lost decade of growth while the energy transition lags behind the geopolitics of fossil fuels.

sections.heading: The Sanctions Loophole: Russian Oil Returns Through the Back Door
sections.body:
As the Iran war tightens global crude supplies, California is importing fuel made from Russian crude oil. The mechanism is the so-called 'refining loophole': oil refined in a third country loses its origin designation under current sanctions regimes. India's Jamnagar refinery—the world's largest—processes Russian crude and exports refined products to the United States. In 2025, more than 9 million barrels arrived in California through this route. Six shipments totaling 149,916 barrels arrived through March 2026.

The US Treasury waived sanctions in March for Russian oil already at sea—an effort to lower global prices. The waiver was extended in April. The Energy Secretary called the move 'pragmatic.' The pragmatism has a cost: by one estimate, California's imports have sent hundreds of millions of dollars to the Kremlin war chest.

The structural issue is that sanctions are not binary. They create incentives for rerouting, re-labeling, and refining in third jurisdictions. The Iran war has created such acute supply stress that the United States is now indirectly funding Russia's war in Ukraine while fighting a war against Iran. The sanctions architecture was designed for a world of abundant supply. That world no longer exists.

South Korea, which received most of its oil through Hormuz, has banned fuel exports to conserve domestic supply. The global refining system is reshuffling: countries with refining capacity and access to non-Gulf crude are becoming critical nodes. Those without both are paying premiums or facing shortages.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Washington would be closing the refining loophole—but that would raise fuel prices in an election year. The trap is that every sanctions workaround undermines the credibility of the sanctions regime. The cost is a foreign policy where energy pragmatism trumps strategic consistency.

sections.heading: Critical Minerals: India Builds a Six-Month Buffer
sections.body:
While the Iran war dominates energy headlines, another supply chain vulnerability is being addressed. India is establishing a six-month strategic stockpile of critical minerals—lithium, cobalt, rare earths—to reduce import dependence and buffer against supply shocks. The reserve will serve sectors including electric mobility, energy storage, and electronics—industries where India has ambitions of scale.

The move reflects a lesson learned from the Hormuz crisis: strategic reserves matter. But the critical minerals problem is different. Rare earth processing is concentrated in China. Cobalt mining in Congo is dominated by Chinese firms. Lithium supply chains run through Chile and Australia but refining capacity sits in Beijing's sphere. Building a stockpile addresses the symptom, not the structural dependency.

China approved a major 60-ton shipment of yttrium oxide—used in aerospace and defense—to the United States in March, exceeding total exports since controls began. The approval signals that Beijing can tighten or loosen the spigot at will. The Quad—US, Japan, India, Australia—is discussing intelligence sharing on critical mineral supply risks. But intelligence does not create supply; it only illuminates vulnerability.

The United States is losing the race for Central Asia's critical minerals to China. The Lobito Railway in Congo—backed by Washington to break Beijing's hold on African metals—remains a work in progress. Meanwhile, China and Turkmenistan signed a deal this month to build the fourth phase of the Galkynysh gas field, deepening energy ties that route through Beijing's infrastructure, not Washington's.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move would be coordinated Western investment in mining, processing, and refining capacity outside China's sphere. The trap is believing that stockpiles and intelligence sharing substitute for industrial policy. The cost is a clean energy transition built on materials controlled by a strategic competitor.

sections.heading: Türkiye's Energy Crossroads: The Iran Contract Expires
sections.body:
Türkiye's long-term contract for importing natural gas from Iran expires in the coming months. According to reporting, no talks on extension have yet begun. The timing is delicate: Tehran is isolated by war and sanctions, and Ankara is recalibrating its energy partnerships.

Türkiye sits on the proposed Iraq-Turkey pipeline route that IEA head Fatih Birol has pitched as a Hormuz bypass—linking Basra's oil fields to Ceyhan on the Mediterranean. The Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline is already operating at 250,000 barrels daily. Expanding it could offer Iraq an export route that doesn't pass through Hormuz. But expansion requires capital, security, and political agreement between Baghdad, Erbil, and Ankara—no small feat.

Türkiye's energy mix is diversifying. TANAP brings Azeri gas to Europe through Turkish territory. The Czech Republic is negotiating to buy up to 2 billion cubic meters annually from Azerbaijan by 2028–29, some of which would transit Turkish infrastructure. Turkmenistan—the world's fourth-largest gas reserves—has re-emerged as a potential supplier to Türkiye, though Ashgabat's focus remains on exports to China.

Türkiye's structural position is that of an energy corridor: not a major producer, but a critical transit route between Asian supply and European demand. The Iran war creates both risk (energy prices, regional instability) and opportunity (alternative pipeline routes that bypass Hormuz through Turkish territory).
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Ankara is positioning as the reliable transit corridor for Gulf and Caspian energy to Europe. The trap is overreaching—demanding transit fees or political concessions that push energy flows to other routes. The cost of miscalculation is losing the infrastructure investments that energy geography offers.

bottomLine: The Iran war has accomplished something decades of analysis could not: it proved that energy security architecture built on maritime chokepoints is not a system but a gamble. The Gulf's pipeline workarounds are operating at capacity and still leave a 15-million-barrel daily gap. OPEC has lost its second-largest Gulf producer to a strategic walkout. Iran is being squeezed by the same strait it weaponized. Europe is inching toward stagflation. Russian oil flows to California through Indian refineries while Washington fights Tehran. The lesson is structural: the energy transition is not just about climate—it is about breaking dependencies on geography, cartels, and regimes that can, at any moment, turn off the lights.
sources: AP News, Reuters, The Conversation, New Arab, USA Today/Capital & Main, MSN, TASS, Interfax, Outlook India, Investor Ideas, Korea Herald, Azernews, Eurasi.net, Bloomberg
